_about: Cross-deck SYNTHESIS for each Major Arcanum — the evolution narrative across all decks. Keyed by trump_key. Consumed by (1) scripts/build_meta_grammar.py, which injects it into the emergent num-major-N nodes as an 'Across the decks' section, and (2) viewers/prototypes/lenses.html, which renders it as an overview header. Add a trump here and both surfaces pick it up. Stubs left empty fall back to the auto-generated one-liner.
fool: Across the decks, the Fool travels further than any other card — not in its picture, which stays remarkably stable, but in its rank and meaning. The figure is recognisable across five centuries: a wanderer in motley with a staff and bundle over his shoulder and an animal at his legs. The **Visconti-Sforza** (1451) shows him as a ragged youth, feathers in his hair and tattered clothing — the 'wild man' or madman of medieval iconography. The **Marseille** (1760) fixes the canonical image: the parti-coloured vagrant striding to the card's edge, a dog tearing at his leg. In the card *game* he was the **excuse** — the one card belonging to no suit, worth almost nothing, yet playable at any time to escape following suit. Then the meaning inverts. **Court de Gébelin** (1781) reads the unnumbered Fool as **Zero** — nothing in itself, yet the cipher that gives the other cards their value. The English occultists complete the turn: in the **Golden Dawn** and Rider-Waite-Smith (1909) the same motley youth now stands at the edge of a cliff, a white rose in hand and a white dog at his heels, about to step into the abyss — innocence, the soul before its descent, the alpha and omega of the Major Arcana. The card that was worth nothing in a game became the spiritual zero-point of an esoteric cosmos, while keeping its dog, its bundle, and its forward step the whole way.
death: Death is the clearest case of an image holding while its meaning is rewritten. The skeleton is constant; everything around it shifts. The oldest deck, the **Cary-Yale** (1442), shows Death *mounted, carrying a bow* — the archer who shoots down the living, rendered with courtly gold-ground dignity in the tradition of Petrarch's *Triumph of Death*. By the printed **Marseille** (1760) the attribute has changed to the **scythe**: Death the reaper, mowing a field from which crowned heads, hands and feet sprout — and the card is numbered **XIII but left unnamed**, a printers' convention. The meaning across the early decks is the same blunt one, shared with the popular *danse macabre*: Death is the great leveller, sparing no rank. Then the occultists transform it. **Court de Gébelin** (1781) reads the blank name as cosmic significance ('Death belongs to no rank'); **Wirth** and the **Golden Dawn** (1889–1909) re-key the card to Scorpio and the Hebrew letter Nun, insist it means **transformation, not extinction**, and put Death back on a *horse* — now with a banner promising sunrise. Mounted to on-foot to mounted; bow to scythe to banner; mortal allegory to great leveller to esoteric rebirth — the figure stayed recognisable for five centuries while its meaning was completely remade.
